coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on molybdenum-doped carbon dots nanozyme for visual monitoring of ampicillin
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Wenjing
Guo, Yanjiao
Yue, Yongfang
Zhang, Jinghua
Fan, Li
Li, Feng
Zhao, Yi
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring and control of ampicillin (AMP) residues in environment are of great significance for reducing bacterial resistance, maintaining microecological balance, and protecting human health. In this work, an innovative molybdenum-doped carbon dots (Mo-CDs) nanozyme was designed and synthesized via one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Due to the suitable selection of precursors, the carbon dots were doped with variable valence Mo, resulting in excellent peroxide-like activity to catalyze H2O2 to 1O2 (K m = 0.176 mM of H2O2). Based on the considerable reactivity between AMP and generated 1O2 of the catalytic system, a colorimetric sensor for AMP detection was constructed with a good linear relationship from 0.0500 to 100 μg/mL, and detection limit down to 12 ng/mL. Furthermore, the visual sensing platform of colorimetric test strips integrated with smartphone were established and successfully realized rapid, portable, and sensitive visual monitoring of AMP in water samples. This work has not only developed a new and efficient carbon dots nanozyme and constructed a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensor, but also proposed a novel design strategy for developing a low-cost and easy-to-operate antibiotic detection tool with good specificity and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum-doped carbon dots
Peroxidase-like nanozyme
Ampicillin
Colorimetric sensing
Smartphone-assisted

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Smartphone-assisted ampicillin detection using test strips
Detection of ampicillin in real samples
Ampicillin detection based on Mo-CDs platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research project support by the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No. 2020-003), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and 81901814 ), and the Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project. The authors also would like to thank Wei Yue from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the ICP-OES analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of the Mo-CDs (inset: HR-TEM); (B) the size distribution of the Mo-CDs; (C) FT-IR spectrum of the Mo-CDs; and (D) XPS spectrum of the Mo-CDs; HR-XPS spectrum of the C 1 s (E); and the Mo 3d of the Mo-CDs (F).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of (A) Mo-CDs + TMB + H2O2, Mo-CDs + H2O2, Mo-CDs + TMB, H2O2 + TMB, Mo-CDs, H2O2 and TMB; The impact of (B) the content of Mo-CDs; (C) pH; (D) temperature and (E) time on POD-like activity of Mo-CDs; (F) The catalytic stability evaluation of Mo-CDs for different time.
Steady-state kinetic assay of Mo-CDs for TMB (A) and H2O2 (B); Lineweaver − Burk curve of Mo-CDs for TMB (C) and H2O2 (D).

(A) The relative POD-like activity of Mo-CDs catalytic system with ROS scavengers (IPA, PBQ and NaN3, 5 mM; Mo-CDs: 0.10 mg/mL, TMB:1.0 mM, H2O2: 0.5 mM, pH 4.0); (B) EPR spectra of Mo-CDs + H2O2 + TEMP/DMPO; (C) EPR spectra of Mo-CDs + TEMP, H2O2 + TEMP, and Mo-CDs + H2O2 + TEMP; (D) The relative absorption of DPBF with different treatments.

(A) The selectivity of Mo-CDs for penicillin antibiotics detection (AMP, PG, PVK, AMX, 0.3 mg/mL, respectively, and NA, NFX, LVLX, ERY, OTC, SM, MTZ, ONZ, TNZ, 1.0 mg/mL, respectively); (B) The UV–Vis absorption variation of the sensing system with different concentration of AMP; (C) Relationship between the ΔA652nm and AMP concentration. Inset: the linear calibration plot for detection of AMP.

(A) Images of different concentrations of AMP and Mo-CDs sensing test paper after reaction in visible light; (B) Plot of the relative color information (L/S) versus concentration of AMP on the test paper.
Illustration of smartphone-assisted Mo-CDs nanozyme sensing platform for colorimetric analysis of AMP.
Determination results of AMP content in water samples.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on molybdenum-doped carbon dots nanozyme for visual monitoring of ampicillin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Office of Clinical Trial Institution, The Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Office of Clinical Trial Institution, The Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring and control of ampicillin (AMP) residues in environment are of great significance for reducing bacterial resistance, maintaining microecological balance, and protecting human health. In this work, an innovative molybdenum-doped carbon dots (Mo-CDs) nanozyme was designed and synthesized via one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Due to the suitable selection of precursors, the carbon dots were doped with variable valence Mo, resulting in excellent peroxide-like activity to catalyze H2O2 to 1O2 (K m = 0.176 mM of H2O2). Based on the considerable reactivity between AMP and generated 1O2 of the catalytic system, a colorimetric sensor for AMP detection was constructed with a good linear relationship from 0.0500 to 100 μg/mL, and detection limit down to 12 ng/mL. Furthermore, the visual sensing platform of colorimetric test strips integrated with smartphone were established and successfully realized rapid, portable, and sensitive visual monitoring of AMP in water samples. This work has not only developed a new and efficient carbon dots nanozyme and constructed a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensor, but also proposed a novel design strategy for developing a low-cost and easy-to-operate antibiotic detection tool with good specificity and sensitivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have gained worldwide attention for their ubiquitous detrimental effects on the natural environment. For a long time, antibiotics have been used extensively and unreasonably in medical treatment, animal husbandry, aquaculture and other fields. Global antibiotic consumption reached 34.8 billion defined daily doses by 2015 and is going to double by 2030 [1,2]. The increasing use of antibiotics has led to their widespread presence in rivers around the world. Rapid and accurate monitoring of antibiotic concentration is of great significance to realize early warning of water sample risk. Ampicillin (AMP) as one of the most frequently used penicillin antibiotics in the world have been widely used in the prevention and treatment of various bacterial infections in human, livestock and aquaculture [3,4]. The pollution problems of these antibiotic residues are becoming more and more prominent. Antibiotic residues can enhance bacterial resistance, destroy human and animal gastrointestinal tract and microecological balance [5–7]. Therefore, the monitoring and control of antibiotic residues in environment are very important.

Presently, existing methods for the determination of antibiotic residues mainly include traditional microbial inhibition method, high-performance liquid chromatography, enzyme-linked immunoassay, surface plasmon resonance, etc [8,9]. These methods have the disadvantages of time-consuming, cumbersome operation, expensive instruments and low sensitivity. To address these limitations, various sensors including electrochemical, Raman, and fluorescence have been constructed to detect AMP [10–12]. For example, Yan et al. [13] constructed a novel aptamer sensor for FRET-based detection between UCNP and AuNPs and applied it to the analysis of AMP in milk. Xu’s group [14] proposed an electrochemical sensor to detect AMP, which used a mesoporous In2O3@Au nanofiber incubated with the aptamer as the working electrode. However, these sensors usually required an aptamer as the identification unit of the object under test, which not only increased the difficulty of material preparation but also resulted in poor reproducibility. Therefore, developing a sensitive and simple rapid detection method to overcome the above limitations is very necessary.

According to previous studies, the β-lactam antibiotics are prone to oxidative degradation due to the existence of some electron-rich groups. Therefore, researchers have developed novel methods for the oxidative degradation of these antibiotics using a variety of oxidation-active species (such as KMnO4, SO4
−, ozone, ferrate (VI) and so on) [15–17]. Based on the inspirations above, it is feasible to construct a method for the specific detection of β-lactam antibiotics by combining catalytic reactions that can generate reactive oxygen species (such as H2O2, O2 −, 1O2, and OH), with chromogenic agents. In recent years, catalytic oxidation reactions involving nanozymes have shown excellent application potential in biomedicine, chemical sensing, environmental detection and other fields [18–20]. Many studies have reported that constructing sensors to detect small biological molecules and pesticides based on the color reaction between the nanozymes with peroxidase or oxidase-like activity and substrate [21–23]. Lu et al. [24] reported an intelligent detection platform based on SA-Co/NC single-atom catalysts with oxidase-like activity and realized the visualization monitoring the total antioxidant capacity of skin care products. Among many nanozyme materials, carbon dots (CDs) stood out because of their advantages including excellent nano size, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility and stability. Besides, their unique preponderances of simple preparation, diverse precursor sources and rich structural characteristics provide more possibilities for the development of nanozymes with outstanding catalytic performance [25–27]. In recent years, with the continuous upgrade of smartphones equipped with HD cameras, smartphone has received wide attention as a good analysis device in point-of-care testing (POCT) sensors. So far, multiple colorimetric detection platforms based on smartphones have been reported [28–30]. Therefore, how to build an integrated intelligent detection platform to realize sensitive, accurate and convenient in-situ monitoring of AMP by combining nanozyme reaction with smartphone in a simple way is of great significance.

With the above considerations, here we synthesized the molybdenum-doped carbon dots (Mo-CDs) through a simple hydrothermal synthesis process. Mo5+ doped of Mo-CDs could convert H2O2 into 1O2 through the Russell mechanism, showing excellent peroxide-like activity. Based on the Mo-CDs nanozyme catalytic system, a colorimetric sensor for the detection of AMP have been developed. In the presence of AMP, it could compete with 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the catalytic system to generate 1O2, thus inhibiting the color reaction of TMB (Scheme 1 ). In addition, based on the above analysis methods, the colorimetric test strips were developed and an integrated sensing platform combining with smartphone was constructed to realize rapid, sensitive and accurate visual monitoring of AMP in water samples. This strategy not only expanded the application range of CDs nanozyme, but also provided a low-cost, portable, and visual monitoring platform for on-site detection of antibiotics.

The Mo-CDs were synthesized via the one-pot hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.61 g of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and 0.70 g of ascorbic acid (AA) were sufficiently dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water by ultrasound for 10 min. The completely dissolved solution was transferred to a teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the products were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min and filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove deposits. Finally, the Mo-CDs were dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500) and collected by freeze-drying. In addition, the carbon dots without molybdenum-doped (CDs) were prepared by the same procedure with Mo-CDs using only ascorbic acid as only precursor.

In the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), the peroxidase-like activity of Mo-CDs was assessed by catalytic oxidation of the TMB to ox-TMB. In brief, 20 μL of Mo-CDs (10 mg/mL), 20 μL of H2O2 (50 mM) and 40 μL of TMB (25 mM) were added into the 1920 μL of acetate buffer (pH 4, 0.2 M) and reacted at 40 °C for 10 min, recorded the color changes of solution and the UV–Vis absorption changes of TMB at 652 nm. In addition, the effect of Mo-CDs concentration (0.025–0.25 mg/mL), pH (2.0–7.0), reaction temperature (20–80 °C), and reaction time (1.0–30 min) on the peroxidase-like activity was evaluated using the same procedures.

Using TMB or H2O2 as substrate, the kinetics assays of the Mo-CDs nanozyme were analyzed. Briefly, various concentrations of H2O2 (or TMB) were added to mixed solution containing 0.1 mg/mL Mo-CDs and 0.5 mM TMB (or 0.25 mM H2O2). All reactions were reacted at 40 °C for 10 min, and the A652 nm of each sample was measured. Used the following equation to calculate the reaction rate of the Mo-CDs through the Lineweaver Burk double reciprocal graph:
1 / V =
K m
/
V
max
[ S ]
+ 1 /
V
max
, Where, K m, [S], V and V max represent Michaelis-Menten constant, substrate concentration, the initial reaction velocity and maximum reaction velocity, respectively.

For ESR measurement: The singlet oxygen (1O2) generated was detected by 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) trapping agent. The hydroxyl radical (OH) or superoxide anion (O2 −) generated was detected by 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) trapping agent. Usually, TEMP (0.25 M dissolved in ethanol) was added to the Mo-CDs (0.1 mg/mL), H2O2 (5 mM) or Mo-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) + H2O2 (5 mM) solution. After 10 min reaction, the EPR spectra were recorded. The trapping agents of OH and O2 − were DMPO (0.25 M, in ethanol) and DMPO (0.25 M, in methanol), respectively. The experimental process was the same as above.

In addition, the 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a 1O2 probe was used to detect the 1O2 generation. Mo-CDs (0.1 mg/mL), 1.0 mM H2O2 and 20 μg/mL DPBF were incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4). The absorption values of samples at 419 nm were detected.

The colorimetric detection signals were based on the changes of the absorption peak of the ox-TMB at 652 nm. Briefly, the sensing system was set as follows: various concentrations of AMP were added to the HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4, 0.2 M) containing Mo-CDs (0.1 mg/mL), 0.5 mM H2O2 and 0.5 mM TMB. The above series of solutions were reacted at 40 °C for 10 min, and the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the resulting solution were recorded under 652 nm, and the LOD was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted ampicillin detection using test strips
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve on-site detection, the smartphone-assisted measurement of AMP based on low-cost test strips was designed. First, the filter paper was cut into 1 cm × 3 cm as the test strips. The Mo-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL, pH 4) was sprayed on the test strip evenly. Then, H2O2 (1.0 mM) and TMB (1.0 mM) solutions were dropped onto a test strip and incubated at room temperature for 10 min. The test strip containing Mo-CDs, H2O2 and TMB showed a blue color. Afterward, different concentrations of AMP or real samples were added onto the test strip. After reacting for 10 min, the color change of the test strips was captured through the smartphone camera. The color parameters of the image were analyzed through the Color Identification APP and converted to the concentration of the sample with the standard curve for the quantitative detection of AMP. All the images were taken by a smartphone with the model HONOR 30S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ampicillin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of the colorimetric detection method, the content of ampicillin in different water samples (Tap water, the Lingde Lake (Shanxi University, China) water and the Fenhe River (Shanxi, China) water) was tested. To reduce the interference of impurities, the water samples were subjected to the following treatment process before analysis. Briefly, each sample was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min, then the supernatant was collected and used 0.22 μm microporous membrane for filtration. Finally, the filtrate was adjusted to pH 4 with the acetate buffer (0.2 M). The colorimetric detection and smartphone imaging sensor were used to measure AMP in real samples. The test process was the same as before.

The molybdenum-doped carbon dots (Mo-CDs) were synthesized by a typical hydrothermal route using (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and ascorbic acid (AA) as precursors. The facile synthesis method of the Mo-CDs had been shown in the experimental section. The Mo-CDs exhibited outstanding dispersibility and high water solubility. Firstly, the morphology and composition of the Mo-CDs were explored. It could be seen from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization that the Mo-CDs were monodisperse spherical particles (Fig. 1 A). The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) picture (the inset of Fig. 1A) revealed that there was the crystalline structure in the Mo-CDs with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which was close to the (100) diffraction planes of graphite [31]. The average diameter of the Mo-CDs was about 3.49 nm (Fig. 1B). The doping content of molybdenum in the Mo-CDs was determined about 18.43% by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES). Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Fig. 1C) was carried out to investigate the characterize functional groups distribution related to Mo-CDs surface, and the results showed that the peaks at 3428, 3233 and 2926–2970 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of O − H, N − H and C − H, respectively [32]. The sharp peaks at 1627 and 1401 cm−1 were attributed to the C = O and C = C, respectively [33]. In addition, the absorption peak at 892 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of Mo-O [34]. As shown in the Fig. 1D, the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) stated four peaks at 232.5, 284.8, 400.0 and 532.2 eV, which indicated the major elements of the Mo-CDs were the Mo, C, N and O, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum was subdivided into four components at 284.2, 284.8, 285.9 and 288.5 eV, which were matched with C − C/C = C, C − N, C − O and C = O, respectively (Fig. 1E) [35].The peaks located at 398.0, 399.2 and 401.3 eV in the N 1 s spectrum indicated the presence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and N-H, respectively (Fig. S1A) [36]. The O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1B) were revealed three peaks at 531.0, 532.2 and 533.3 eV, which represented C − OH/C − O − C, C = O, and metal-bound oxygen, respectively [37]. It could be seen from the results of the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mo 3d (Fig. 1F) that Mo in the Mo-CDs was a mixed valence state of Mo5+ and Mo6+. The peaks at 231.9, 232.3, 235.0 and 235.4 eV were ascribed to Mo5+ 3d5/2, Mo6+ 3d5/2, Mo5+3d3/2 and Mo6+3d3/2, respectively [38,39]. The XPS analysis was consistent with FT-IR results, which further confirmed the successful doping of molybdenum.

Subsequently, the optical properties of Mo-CDs were characterized by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. In the UV − Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S2A), the absorption peak at 226 nm was caused by the π → π* of the sp2 hybridization in the carbon core [40], and the absorption located at 340 nm was attributed to the n → π* of C = O [41]. The Mo-CDs solution showed blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. In the fluorescence spectrum, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength of Mo-CDs were 371 nm and 469 nm, respectively. In addition, due to the existence of various surface states, the photoluminescence behavior of Mo-CDs demonstrated excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. S2B). Furthermore, the quantum yield of the Mo-CDs was about 2.69 % against the reference quinine sulfate.

Using reducing ascorbic acid as a precursor, not only could provide carbon source for the preparation of carbon dots, but also could reduce the Mo6+ in (NH4)6Mo7O24 to Mo5+ during the synthesis process. In addition, XPS results have confirmed the existence of Mo5+ in the Mo-CDs. In addition, the REDOX reaction between Mo with different valence states has showed good application potential in the field of biocatalysis. Therefore, we investigated the biocatalytic performances of Mo-CDs. It is well known that peroxidase can catalyze the oxidation of TMB by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to oxidized-TMB, changing the solution from colorless to blue and producing an absorption peak at 652 nm [42]. Therefore, in the presence of H2O2, a typical TMB oxidation reaction was used as a probe to prove the POD-like activity of Mo-CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 A, negligible ox-TMB absorption change with the Mo-CDs + TMB treated and other control groups could be observed, while an obvious absorption peak appeared at 652 nm could be found in the group of H2O2-assisted Mo-CDs + TMB treatment, and the color of the solution changed from light yellow to blue. This indicated that Mo-CDs had POD-like activity, which could catalyze H2O2 to produce oxidized substances. In addition, we used ascorbic acid as the only precursor to synthesize undoped molybdenum carbon dots (CDs) by the same method, and investigated whether it showed peroxidase property. Compared with Mo-CDs, CDs could not catalyze H2O2 to oxidize TMB, proving that molybdenum doping was the key factor to make Mo-CDs possess POD enzyme (Fig. S3). In order to inspect the selectivity of Mo-CDs as POD enzyme in response to H2O2, s variety of ions, cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), serine (Ser), glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), citric acid (CA), and dopamine (DA) were selected as the interfering substances and verified by colorimetric reaction. The results indicated that compared with other interferents, Mo-CDs displayed excellent selectivity for H2O2 (Fig. S4). Because the stronger the enzyme activity, the faster the enzymatic reaction. There are many factors affecting enzyme activity, mainly including enzyme concentration, pH, temperature and reaction time [43]. Therefore, the effects of different concentrations of the Mo-CDs (0.025–0.25 mg/mL), pH (2.0–7.0), reaction temperature (20–80 °C), and time (1.0–30 min) on the POD-like activity were explored. Fig. 2B showed that as the concentrations of Mo-CDs increased from 0.025 mg/mL to 0.25 mg/mL, the POD-like activity of Mo-CDs first increased and then decreased, achieving the maximum when the concentration was 0.10 mg/mL. Moreover, the catalytic activity of Mo-CDs is also pH and temperature dependent. It can be seen from the results that when the pH and temperature were 4.0 and 40 °C, respectively, the catalytic activity was the highest (Fig. 2C, D). After the reaction time was 10 min, the catalytic activity reached its peak (Fig. 2E). Therefore, the POD-like activity of Mo-CDs was the highest under the following experimental conditions: 0.10 mg/mL Mo-CDs, pH 4.0, 40 °C, incubation for 10 min. In addition, the Mo-CDs had excellent catalytic stability. The morphology and structure of Mo-CDs were basically consistent with the initial state even after 60 days placement. There was no aggregation and metamorphism in the Mo-CDs solution, and the good catalytic activity of Mo-CDs could be maintained for at least 60 days (Fig. 2F and Fig. S5).

Under the optimal conditions, steady state kinetic analysis was carried out TMB and H2O2 as substrates, which was used to systematically study the peroxidase mimic activity of Mo-CDs. It can be observed from Fig. 3 A and B that the catalytic reactions of the two substrates involved in Mo-CDs both conformed to the standard Michaelis-Mending model. The corresponding Michaelis constant (K m) and the maximum initial velocity (V max) of TMB and H2O2 were obtained through the intercept and slope of the fitting line of the Burk double reciprocal graphs (Fig. 3C, D). The K m of TMB and H2O2 were 0.245 mM and 0.176 mM, respectively. Compared with the classical natural peroxidase-horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (0.434 mM for TMB and 3.70 mM for H2O2) [44] and multiple peroxide nanozymes, the Mo-CDs all showed better affinity towards two substrates. Meanwhile, the V max of TMB and H2O2 were 3.20 × 10−8 M s−1 and 4.76 × 10−8 M s−1, which were higher than most other peroxidases, indicating that the Mo-CDs as a new type of nanozyme had excellent catalytic rate (Table S1).

Herein, to investigate the catalytic mechanism of the Mo-CDs nanozyme, we evaluated the type of ROS produced during the catalytic reaction. First, the sodium azide (NaN3), 1, 4-Benzoquinone (PBQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were used as scavengers for singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion radical (O2 −), and hydroxyl radical (OH), respectively [45]. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, compared with the absence of reactive oxygen scavenger and the addition of other two scavengers, the POD-like activity of Mo-CDs decreased significantly, when NaN3 was added. This indicated that the dominant ROS was 1O2 in the process of catalyzed oxidation of H2O2 by Mo-CDs nanozyme. Furthermore, we qualitatively confirmed the generation of 1O2 using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. Using DMPO and TEMP as trapping agents, Mo-CDs nanozyme could only produce EPR signal peaks of 1O2, but could not produce O2 − and OH in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 4B). And as shown in Fig. 4C, only when Mo-CDs + H2O2 + TEMP coexist, three strong characteristic peaks would appear with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1. Moreover, the 1, 3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was also used to verify the 1O2 production according to the 1O2-driven DPBF degradation (Fig. 4D and Fig. S6), which further confirmed that Mo-CDs could catalyze H2O2 to generate 1O2. It was depicted that the 1O2 formation can be attributed to the Russell mechanism. Due to the existence of Mo5+, the Mo-CDs reacted with H2O2 and formed ROO groups via oxidation. Subsequently, every two ROO groups were combined to produce a transition-state product, which was then rearranged to produce 1O2 and the by-product 3O2 (ground state oxygen). As a result, Mo5+ was oxidized back to Mo6+ [38,39,46]. In summary, the excellent POD-like activity of Mo-CDs depended on the doping of Mo5+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ampicillin detection based on Mo-CDs platform
ce:section.ce:para:
Because the β-lactam antibiotics possessed considerable reactivity with various oxidants and free radicals (such as SO4
−, OH, 1O2 and O2 −) [15–17]. Consequently, it is of great significance to develop a reliable β-lactam antibiotics detection system based on this reaction strategy. On account of the Mo-CDs could convert H2O2 to 1O2 through the Russell mechanism in the presence of H2O2. So we explored the potential of Mo-CDs catalytic system in the detection of AMP. The Mo-CDs with POD-like activity can oxidize TMB to ox-TMB, thus generating a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm and turning the solution into blue. In the presence of AMP, 1O2 produced in the reaction reacted with it, which caused the absorption peak at 652 nm to decrease and color of the solution to fade. As depicted in Fig. 5 A and Fig. S7, compared to multiple ions, amino acids, and different kinds of antibiotics, the above sensing system had good selectivity to penicillin antibiotics and the best response to AMP. Hence, we could construct a colorimetric sensing platform for the detection of AMP based on Mo-CDs nanozyme. In order to evaluate the sensitivity and linear range of AMP detection, various concentrations of AMP standard solutions were added to the reaction system. With the increased of AMP content, the A652nm of the sensor system gradually decreased under the optimized conditions (Fig. 5B). There was an outstanding linear relationship between the A652nm and the concentration of AMP, ranging from 0.0500 to 100 μg/mL (ΔA652nm = 0.00471[AMP] + 0.00581, R2 = 0.97), with a limit of detection of 12 ng/mL (S/N = 3) (Fig. 5C), which performance was better than many other AMP sensors (Table S2). According to the above results, the colorimetric sensing platform based on Mo-CDs manifested excellent selectivity and sensitivity in monitoring AMP.

In order to expand the sensor strategy to a wider range of application scenarios, to achieve a more simple and portable on-situ detection, we innovatively designed AMP visual quantitative test strips based on the Mo-CDs. Firstly, the test strips were prepared by spraying of Mo-CDs (0.1 mg/mL), H2O2 (1.0 mM) and TMB (1.0 mM) in acetate buffer solution (pH 4), which showed the blue color under visible light. Secondly, when AMP was driped on the surface of the test paper, an obvious color change could be observed with the naked eye after 10 min. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the different concentrations would lead to the change of distinguishable color. Simultaneously, we combined the portable test strip with the accurate color recognition function of a smartphone to construct an ampicillin detection platform that could realize on-site and visual monitoring. First, the smartphone camera immediately captured a series of AMP test papers. Then, the software (Color Identification) recognized the color of the acquired image and converted it into Hue (H), Saturation (S), and Lightness (L) values. Finally, in order to ensure the accuracy of the method, the linear relationship of AMP quantitative detection was established with the ratio of L to S (L/S) as the ordinate and the concentration of AMP on test paper as the abscissa. As displayed in Fig. 6B, L/S and AMP concentrations (0.500 to 100 μg/mL) revealed a good linear relationship, and the linear regression equation was L/S = 0.0585[AMP] + 0.1810 (R2 = 0.99), with the LOD of 0.14 μg/mL which proved that the smartphone aided sensing platform could achieve efficient and highly sensitive AMP detection.

To demonstrate the effectiveness and reliability of the developed method, tap water, Lingde Lake water and Fenhe River were collected and analyzed. The colorimetric detection and the smartphone-assisted sensing platform was used to detect the AMP in actual water samples. The AMP concentration values were obtained using the calibration curve in Fig. 5C and 6B. The recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the sensor were calculated by adding different concentrations of AMP to the water sample. As presented in Table 1 , based on the colorimetric method, the recovery of AMP in the actual sample was 95.34–104.8%, and the RSD was lower than 4.77%. At the same time, the recovery and RSD of AMP based on smartphone imaging sensors were 94.66–105.8% and 2.55–6.72%, respectively. The colorimetric detection and smartphone imaging sensor demonstrate basically the same results. Additionally, the same samples were further investigated using a typical high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) technique with a UV detector as the standard method for was used to evaluate the accuracy of the detection results. The linear equation between the concentration of AMP and integral area was constructed with a linear range from 0.25 to 10 μg/mL for AMP detection (Fig. S8). The analysis results are displayed in Table S3, the recovery of AMP in the actual sample was 96.48–104.6%, and the RSD was 1.48–4.11%. The results obtained by the colorimetric detection and smartphone imaging sensor coincided with that acquired by the HPLC-UV method, which proved the two analytical strategies developed based on Mo-CDs with high accuracy and reliability, and could be used to detect AMP in environmental water.

In summary, a new type of Mo-doped carbon dots were prepared, which showed excellent peroxide-like properties due to its unique structural characteristics, and could generate singlet oxygen by catalytic oxidation of H2O2 through the Russell mechanism. In addition, a novel colorimetric detection method for ampicillin was constructed based on the reaction between ROS produced by Mo-CDs nanozyme and ampicillin. Simultaneously, combining the colorimetric test strips with smartphone, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform was developed to realize the convenient and fast detection of AMP in water samples. The results displayed that the colorimetric detection method and the integrated test strip had the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity and stability for the detection of AMP, which confirmed the feasibility of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform and was expected to be an ideal antibiotic detection tool. The preparation and development of the Mo-CDs not only explored its application potential as a nanocatalyst in environmental monitoring, but also provided a good strategy for the development of a convenient and on-site intelligent detection platform.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research project support by the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No. 2020-003), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and 81901814), and the Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project. The authors also would like to thank Wei Yue from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the ICP-OES analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.143615.

ce:section.ce:para:
